critic_raw:
## Analysis

This is a carefully designed methodology with genuine strengths: temporal splitting, single test-set evaluation, preprocesing fitted only on training data, and a well-specified baseline. Most design choices are defensible. I found a small number of genuine issues worth raising.

---

### Issue 1: The 24-hour contact-intensity score is a strong candidate for label leakage

**Claim:** The rolling 24-hour account contact-intensity score is 'available at prediction time' and safe to include as a feature.

**Mechanism of failure:** The methodology states this score 'summarizes support touches and routing events associated with the customer.' Urgent cases are—by definition—cases that receive elevated handling: escalations, callbacks, follow-up contacts. A customer whose ticket will be escalated within 2 hours is likely generating routing events and follow-up contacts during that same 24-hour window that partly overlap with the events used to construct the label. The question is not whether the score exists at prediction time, but whether the score's value, as computed from logged data, incorporates any events that occur *after* the ticket opens but before the label is assigned. If the score is computed from the raw event log at batch time (as is common in historical dataset construction), then for any ticket opened at T=0 and escalated at T=1.5h, the 24-hour window used to compute the score at construction time may include the escalation routing events themselves. This is a construction-time leakage, not a deployment-time leakage, and it would be invisible in offline evaluation while producing inflated results in production.

**Evidence:** The test would be to ablate this feature and observe whether model performance drops substantially (>3–4 F1 points) on the test set—a drop disproportionate to its feature importance rank relative to other structured features. Alternatively, recompute the score using a strict T=0 cutoff for all tickets in the dataset and re-evaluate. If scores differ materially for urgent vs. non-urgent cases in a way that vanishes when the strict cutoff is applied, leakage is confirmed.

---

### Issue 2: The baseline is not a fair test of the history hypothesis—it conflates model class with feature set

**Claim:** The logistic regression baseline 'isolates the effect of history' because it uses the same DistilBERT embedding pipeline but no historical features, and 'any improvement is attributable to joint encoding, not model class complexity.'

**Mechanism of failure:** This is not correct. The proposed model is a transformer-based joint encoder with a 2-layer MLP head, fine-tuned end-to-end (implied by the shared DistilBERT encoder being trained jointly). The baseline is logistic regression on frozen DistilBERT embeddings. There are two simultaneous changes: (a) history features added, and (b) a fine-tuned MLP decoder vs. a frozen linear decoder. Any observed improvement conflates these two factors. If the MLP fine-tuning alone on current-message features substantially outperforms the logistic regression baseline, we cannot attribute the delta to history. The methodology requires an additional ablation: current-message-only MLP (same architecture as proposed model, no history features) vs. proposed model with history. Without this, the comparison does not isolate the claimed mechanism.

**Evidence:** Train the proposed architecture (DistilBERT + MLP) on current-message-only features. If this already closes most of the gap vs. the full model, history is not contributing meaningfully. If the full model retains a significant margin over this matched ablation, the history hypothesis is supported.

---

### Issue 3: Inter-rater filtering on 500 samples does not validate label quality—it selects easy cases

**Claim:** Tickets with <80% inter-rater agreement are excluded, eliminating 'adversarially noisy examples that would corrupt model training.'

**Mechanism of failure:** The filtering has two problems. First, 500 samples out of 500k+ is 0.1% of the data. The estimated disagreement rate in this 0.1% sample cannot be used to locate and exclude disagreed-upon examples in the remaining 99.9% of the data—those examples are never re-labeled and remain in the dataset. So the filtering applies only to 500 tickets, not to the full training set. The exclusion policy as described has negligible effect on actual label noise at scale.

Second, the 80% threshold for exclusion means that cases where experts disagree are removed. Those are precisely the genuinely ambiguous cases the model will encounter in production. A model trained without ambiguous examples may be well-calibrated on clear-cut cases but confidently miscalibrated on the hard cases that matter most operationally. The ECE metric will not catch this if ambiguous cases are also rare in the test set (which they will be, since they come from the same distribution).

**Evidence:** Estimate the noise rate from the 500-sample audit. If it is >10%, the model should be re-evaluated with a noise-robust loss (e.g., label smoothing or symmetric cross-entropy) as a control. To test whether ambiguous-case exclusion harms calibration, hold out the disagreed-upon 500 tickets as a separate probe set and report calibration on them specifically.

---

### Summary

The three issues above are real methodological concerns, not hedges. Issue 1 (leakage via the contact-intensity score) is the most dangerous because it could silently inflate offline metrics in a way that does not transfer to production. Issue 2 (confounded baseline) is the most important for the scientific validity of the history-contribution claim. Issue 3 (inter-rater filtering scope) overstates the noise mitigation actually performed.

The remaining design choices—temporal split, single test evaluation, preprocessing discipline, threshold freezing, stratified reporting—are sound.
all_issues_raised: The 24-hour contact-intensity score may encode post-ticket-open routing events that overlap with the escalation label, creating construction-time label leakage invisible in offline evaluation.
The baseline conflates history features with model class (logistic regression vs. fine-tuned MLP), so gains cannot be attributed to history alone without a matched current-message-only MLP ablation.
Inter-rater filtering applies to only 500 of 500k+ tickets and removes ambiguous cases from evaluation, understating noise impact and potentially producing overconfident models on hard cases.
all_issues_adjudicated: The 24-hour contact-intensity score may encode post-ticket-open routing events that overlap with the escalation label, creating construction-time label leakage invisible in offline evaluation.
The baseline conflates history features with model class (logistic regression vs. fine-tuned MLP), so gains cannot be attributed to history alone without a matched current-message-only MLP ablation.
Inter-rater filtering applies to only 500 of 500k+ tickets and removes ambiguous cases from evaluation, understating noise impact and potentially producing overconfident models on hard cases.
